relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Paul’s freedom and apostleship are bound to seeing the Lord and serving the community.
Corinth itself seals his apostolic work.
His defense begins with apostolic rights.
Eating and drinking could rightly be supported.
Apostolic partnership and family support are real rights.
Paul and Barnabas too could refrain from labor.
Soldier, vineyard, and flock all show workers sharing fruit.
Law also speaks to this principle.
The unmuzzled ox becomes wisdom about labor and provision.
Hope belongs to plowing and threshing.
Spiritual sowing could rightly receive material support.
Paul does not use the right so nothing hinders the good news.
Temple service shares in temple provision.
Good-news workers may live from the good news.
Paul chooses not to use this right.
Good news is necessity, not ego-boast.
The work is stewardship entrusted to him.
His wage is making the good news freely available.
Free from all, he makes himself servant to all.
He adapts to Judeans and those under Law for their gaining.
He adapts to those without Law while remaining within Messiah’s law.
He becomes weak with the weak to save/heal some.
He shares in the good news by serving it.
Run so as to take hold.
Discipline seeks an imperishable crown.
His running and boxing are purposeful.
The body is trained for service so the proclaimer stays aligned.

contemplative_note: 1 Corinthians 9 turns freedom into loving self-limitation. The mature one does not ask only what is permitted, but what serves saving/healing.
